summary:
The Society of Jesus (Jesuits), founded by Ignatius of Loyola and formally approved by Pope Paul III in 1540, was the most important new religious order of the Counter-Reformation and the primary Catholic instrument for reversing [[event:102|Protestant]] gains in Europe and spreading Christianity globally. Ignatius, a Basque noble turned soldier turned mystic after a leg wound at the Battle of Pamplona (1521), developed his Spiritual Exercises during years of ascetic reflection — a structured program of prayer and meditation that became the foundation of Jesuit spiritual formation and one of the most widely used spiritual texts in Western Christianity. The Society he founded was deliberately different from medieval monastic orders: Jesuits did not recite the Divine Office in common, wore no distinctive habit, lived in the world rather than monasteries, and swore a special fourth vow of obedience to the Pope for special missions.

The Jesuit educational mission rapidly became the most significant in Catholic Europe: within decades of their founding, the Jesuits were operating hundreds of colleges and universities from Portugal to Poland, providing free, high-quality humanist education that made them the dominant educational force in Catholic countries for two centuries. Jesuit missionaries showed extraordinary cultural adaptability: Matteo Ricci in China learned Mandarin, dressed as a Confucian scholar, and presented Christianity in terms compatible with Chinese classical culture; Francis Xavier established the first Christian missions in Japan; Jesuits worked in India, Ethiopia, Brazil, and Paraguay (where they created the famous reductions — self-governing indigenous Christian communities that protected their inhabitants from slave-raiding). The Jesuits' political influence in Catholic royal courts eventually made them powerful enemies; they were expelled from Portugal (1759), France (1764), and Spain (1767) and were suppressed by Pope Clement XIV in 1773 — only to be restored in 1814.
